1: Sheba's rebellion-cry: ʾên lānû ḥēleq bᵉdāwiḏ wəlōʾ naḥălāh lānû bᵉben yišay — 'we have no portion (ḥēleq) in David, and we have no inheritance (naḥălāh) in the son of Jesse.' The paired nouns ḥēleq (portion, lot, share) and naḥălāh (inheritance, allotted land) are the covenant-territorial vocabulary of the Promised Land distribution — each tribe's ḥēleq and naḥălāh was their divinely apportioned share in the covenant land. To say 'we have no ḥēleq in David' is to formally renounce membership in the Davidic covenant — the act of self-exclusion from the covenantal community. This formula is repeated verbatim in 1 Kgs 12:16 at the northern kingdom's secession: it becomes the fixed idiom of covenant rupture and dissolution of the Davidic union. In the NT, to have 'no portion in David' becomes the language of inclusion or exclusion from the Davidic Messiah's kingdom (John 13:8: Jesus replies 'if I do not wash you, you have no portion [meros, the LXX term for ḥēleq] with me').
2: Va-ya'al kol-ish Yisra'el me-acharei David acharei Sheva ben-Bikhri — the northern tribes' departure: ya'al (go up) after Sheba. Ve-anshei Yehudah davqu be-malekham min-ha-Yarden ve-ad Yerushalaim — the men of Judah clung (davaq, the covenant-loyalty verb of Ruth 1:14 and Gen 2:24) to their king from the Jordan to Jerusalem. The contrast between northern departure and Judahite adhesion repeats the tribal structure of the Absalom revolt.
3: Va-yavo David el-beito Yerushalaim vayiqach ha-melekh et-eser nashim pilagshim asher hinnich lishemor ha-bayit — David's arrival in Jerusalem and the disposition of the ten concubines. Va-yittenem be-veit mishmereth — he put them in a house of custody (mishmereth), provided for them, but did not go to them. Va-tihnah tzerurot ad yom motanen almanut chayot — they were kept shut up, living in widowhood (almanut) until the day of their death. The women who suffered the public violation become permanent victims of the political crisis they did not choose.
4: Vayomer ha-melekh el-Amasa: zaq-li et-anshei Yehudah shloshet yamim — David's mobilization order to Amasa: muster the men of Judah within three days. The three-day timeline is a tactical deadline — Sheba's rebellion must be suppressed before it can consolidate. Ve-attah po amed — and you, stand here (be here with the assembled force when the time comes).
5: Va-yelekh Amasa liqbots et-Yehudah va-yo'acher mi-ha-moed asher ya'ad lo — Amasa went to muster Judah but was delayed (yo'acher, Hiphil of achar, be late) beyond the set time. The delay is narratively significant: Amasa's inability to mobilize quickly enough gives Joab the pretext to supersede him. The political calculation of replacing Joab with Amasa begins to unravel at the first test.
6: Vayomer David el-Avishai: attah yare' lanu Sheva ben-Bikhri — David's assessment: Sheba will do more harm than Absalom did. Qach et-avdei adonekha ve-radaph acharav — take your lord's servants (Joab's professional force, the Cherethites and Pelethites) and pursue him, lest he find fortified cities and escape. The decision effectively reinstates Joab's force under Abishai's nominal command.
7: Va-yetze'u acharav anshei Yoav ve-ha-Kerethi ve-ha-Pelethi ve-khol-ha-gibborim — the three units following Abishai: Joab's men, the Cherethites and Pelethites (royal guard), and the mighty men (gibborim). The composite force is the professional standing army that has remained loyal throughout the Absalom crisis.
8: Hem im-ha-even ha-gedolah asher be-Givon va-Amasa ba liqratam — Amasa coming to meet them at the great stone in Gibeon. Ve-Yoav chagor milbasho asher livsho ve-alav chagor cherev metzumedeth al-motnav be-ta'aroh — Joab girded with his military garment, with a sword strapped at his hip in its sheath. The detail of Joab's sword setup — the sheath that will allow the blade to fall out when he bends forward — is the narrator's surgical preparation for the murder about to occur.
9: Vayomer Yoav le-Amasa: hashalom attah achi — “Is it well with you, my brother?” Joab's greeting uses the kinship term achi (my brother) — both are David's nephews, making them actual cousins. Shalom before the murder is a bitter irony: the peace-greeting as cover for violence. Va-tochaz yad-Yamin Yoav bi-zeqan Amasa le-nashaq-lo — and Joab's right hand took Amasa by the beard to kiss him. Seizing the beard before a kiss was the gesture of greeting-intimacy; here it positions the victim for the killing blow.
10: Ve-Amasa lo-nishmar ba-cherev asher be-yad Yoav — “and Amasa did not watch (nishmar, Niphal of shamar) the sword that was in Joab's hand.” Va-yakkehuh bah el-ha-chomeish va-yishpokh me'av artzah ve-lo-shanah-lo — he struck him in the belly (chomeish) and poured out his bowels to the ground without striking a second time. The single blow to the abdomen — different from Joab's two-blow killing of Abner (3:27) — is narrated with clinical economy. Joab has now killed two of David's designated commanders, establishing a pattern of unsanctioned eliminations that David cannot address.
11: Ve-ish echad min-na'arei Yoav amad alav vayomer: mi asher chaphetz be-Yoav u-mi asher le-David acharei Yoav — one of Joab's men stood over Amasa's body rallying the troops: “whoever favors Joab and whoever is for David, after Joab!” The juxtaposition of Joab and David as co-loyalties is precisely the political problem: Joab has made himself indispensable by eliminating the alternatives.
12: Ve-Amasa mitgollel ba-dam be-tokh ha-mesilah — Amasa rolling in blood (mitgollel, Hithpoel of galal, to roll) in the middle of the road. Va-yar ha-ish ki-amad kol-ha-am vayaseg et-Amasa min-ha-mesilah ha-sadeh — seeing everyone stopping to look at the body, the man dragged Amasa from the road to the field. The practical problem of a corpse blocking the march is handled with narrative matter-of-factness that heightens the horror.
13: Ka-asher hosar min-ha-mesilah avar kol-ish acharei Yoav lirdoph acharei Sheva ben-Bikhri — once the body was off the road, all the men went after Joab to pursue Sheba. The narrative's restoration of focus to the military mission after Amasa's murder reflects the Succession Narrative's repeated pattern: Joab commits an extrajudicial killing and immediately leads the army back to its task, making his crimes politically unaddressable.
14: Va-ya'avor be-khol shivtei Yisra'el el-Avel u-Veit-Ma'akha ve-khol-ha-Beirim — Sheba's march through all the tribes to Abel-beth-maacah. Avel (Abel) may mean meadow or mourning; beit-Ma'akha (house of Maacah) was a northern city with connections to the Aramaean kingdom of Maacah. Its northern border location makes it Sheba's natural refuge — far from David's base and connected to non-Davidic power.
15: Va-yavo'u va-yatzuru alav be-Avel Beit-Ma'akha — they besieged him in Abel-beth-maacah. Va-yishpeku solelah el-ha-ir va-ta'amod ba-chel — they cast up a siege-ramp (solelah) against the city and it stood against the outer wall (chel). The solelah (siege mound/ramp) is the standard ancient Near Eastern siege technique — documented in Assyrian military reliefs and at Lachish — used to raise the attackers to wall-top level for assault.
16: Va-tiqra ishah chakhamah min-ha-ir: shim'u shim'u imru na el-Yoav qerav ad-henna ve-adabberah elekhah — the wise woman (ishah chakhamah) calling from the city. This is the second ishah chakhamah in the Succession Narrative (cf. 14:2). The pattern of wise women who prevent unnecessary violence is a distinctive feature of 2 Samuel's narrative theology.
17: Va-yiqrav eleha vattomer ha-ishah: ha-attah Yoav vayomer ani — the woman's direct identification of the general before negotiating is a demonstration of authority — she speaks to the commander himself, not through intermediaries.
18: Va-tedabber lemor: dabber yedabberu ba-rishonah lemor: sha'ol yish'alu be-Avel ve-ken hitemmu — “they used to say in former times: let them ask at Abel, and so they would conclude the matter.” The woman invokes Abel's reputation as a place of wise counsel — a city known for resolving disputes without violence. Her appeal to tradition frames the siege as an attack on a resource Israel has historically relied on.
19: Anokhi shelumet emunei Yisra'el attah mevaqesh lehamit ir ve-em be-Yisra'el — “I am one of the peaceable and faithful of Israel; you seek to destroy a city and a mother in Israel.” Ir ve-em be-Yisra'el (a city and a mother in Israel) is a unique phrase — the city as em (mother) is the idiom for a major settlement with dependent villages (cf. Num 21:25). The woman frames the siege as matricide.
20: Vaya'an Yoav vayomer: chalilah chalilah li im-evalla ve-im-ashchit — Joab's denial: chalilah (far be it from me) — the strong negation formula (cf. Gen 44:7; 1 Sam 14:45). The doubled chalilah chalilah intensifies the denial. Joab denies the intent to destroy — he only wants Sheba ben-Bikhri.
21: Lo-khen ha-davar ki ish me-har Ephraim Sheva ben-Bikhri shemo nasa yado ba-melekh be-David — “not so; but a man from the hill country of Ephraim, Sheba ben-Bikhri, has raised his hand against the king.” Nasa yado (raised his hand against) is the idiom of rebellion (cf. 1 Kgs 11:26 for Jeroboam's rebellion). The identification of Sheba as the only target narrows the demand to one person — which is the opening the woman needed.
22: Va-tavo ha-ishah el-kol-ha-am be-chokhmata — the woman going to all the people with her wisdom. Be-chokhmata (in her wisdom) — the noun chokmah used adverbially marks her method. Va-yikhretu et-rosh Sheva ben-Bikhri va-yashlikhu el-Yoav — they cut off Sheba's head and threw it to Joab. Va-yitqa ba-shofar vayafutzu me-al-ha-ir ish le-ohalav — Joab sounded the trumpet and they dispersed from the city, each to his tent. The summary efficiency — one woman's wisdom, one severed head, one trumpet blast — is a pointed contrast to the months of civil war in the Absalom revolt.
23: Ve-Yoav el-kol-ha-tzava Yisra'el — Joab over all the army of Israel. The cabinet list concluding ch. 20 mirrors the one in 8:15–18, framing the restoration as a return to the pre-revolt administrative order. Joab is back in his position — despite having killed Absalom against direct orders and killed Amasa extrajudicially — because there is no one else capable of commanding the army. The political realities of the Davidic state make Joab's accountability impossible within David's lifetime (cf. 1 Kgs 2:5–6).
24: Ve-Adoram al-ha-mas — Adoram over the forced labor (mas). The mas (labor levy, corvée) is a regular institution of David's administrative apparatus — the use of conscripted labor for building projects. Ve-Yehoshaphat ben-Achilud ha-mazkir — Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud, the recorder (mazkir, one who remembers/records — the royal secretary).
25: Ve-Sheva sopher — Sheva (different from Sheba ben-Bikhri) was the secretary (sopher, scribe). Ve-Tzadoq ve-Evyatar kohanim — Zadok and Abiathar as priests. The dual-priesthood that ran through the Absalom crisis remains intact. Ve-gam Ira ha-Ya'iri hayah kohen le-David — and also Ira the Jairite was a priest to David — an additional priestly appointment suggesting the expansion of the Davidic cult administration.
26: Ve-gam Ira ha-Ya'iri hayah kohen le-David — “And also Ira the Jairite was a priest (kohen) to David.” Ira the Jairite appears only in this cabinet list (also at 23:38 among the mighty men). The title kohen le-David (priest to David) is unusual — it may designate a personal royal chaplain or cultic advisor outside the main Zadok-Abiathar priestly structure. In 1 Kgs 4:5, kohen is used for Solomon's personal advisor Zabud (“priest and king's friend”), suggesting a recognized role of personal religious counsel to the king distinct from the official cult.
